Isolation of Mitochondria from Ascites Tumor 
Cells Permeabilized with Digitonin 
RANDALLW.MOREADITHANDGARY FISKUM* 
Department of Phwiological Chemistry The Johns Hopkins Univer&~~ School Q/ Medicine, and 
*Department yf‘Biochemistry, The George Whshington Llniversit)~ Medical Center, Whshington D C 20037 
Received July 15 1983 
A new improved procedure for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells is described 
The unique feature of this technique is the use of digitonin to make the cells susceptible to 
disruption by Teflon pestle/glass vessel homogenization The yield and respiratory control ratios 
of mitochondria isolated by this method from murine Ehrlich ascites tumor cells and rat AS30- 
D ascites hepatoma cells are significantly better than those obtained for mitochondria isolated 
by the commonly employed Nagarse method which involves the use of proteolytic enzymes 
Moreover, mitochondria isolated by this new procedure from three different lines of tumors 
exhibit respiratory control ratios with both adenosine diphosphate and a respiratory uncoupler 
comparable to those obtained with mitochondria present in .situ within digitonin-permeabilized 
tumor cells 
KEY WORDS: tumor; mitochondria: isolation; digitonin; cell: permeabilization 
Tumor cells possess a variety of abnormal 
metabolic characteristics Some of these, such 
as decreased regulatory coupling between gly- 
colysis and oxidative phosphorylation, have 
been attributed to alterations of mitochondrial 
activities (1) These considerations have led 
many investigators to study the respiratory 
and energy-dependent activities of isolated tu- 
mor mitochondria Ascites tumor cells have 
been the most commonly employed tissue for 
these studies because they are highly malignant 
and are quite simple to propagate and harvest 
Several procedures for the isolation of mi- 
tochondria from ascites tumor cells have been 
described Each of these has attempted to deal 
with the problem of the high resistance of these 
cells to disruption and homogenization Re- 
ported methods for effective homogenization 
include grinding with glass beads (2) Dounce 
homogenization (2) nitrogen cavitation (3) 
sonication (4) and proteolytic digestion by 
Nagarse followed by Potter-Elvehjem ho- mogenization (5-7) Each of these, including 
the most commonly used Nagarse method 
have been criticized on the grounds that such 
harsh procedures produce variable degrees of 
mitochondrial damage ( I ,8) However, the 
extent of damage has been difficult to assess 
due to lack of information regarding respi- 
ratory characteristics of ascites cell mitochon- 
dria as they function in situ 
This report describes a new, relatively gentle 
method for disrupting ascites tumor cells by 
permeabilizing them with the steroid glycoside 
digitonin (9) Mitochondria isolated by this 
procedure exhibit a higher yield and higher 
respiratory control ratios than those obtained 
by the Nagarse procedure Experiments are 
also described in which the respiratory control 
ratios of mitochondria present within digi- 
tonin-permeabilized tumor cells are deter- 
mined These values were found to be signif- 
icantly higher than those obtained with iso- 
lated mitochondria prepared by the Nagarse

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** Isolation of Mitochondria from Ascites Tumor Cells Permeabilized with Digitonin

**Authors:** Randall W. Moreadith and Gary Fiskum

**Affiliations:** Department of Physiological Chemistry, The Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine; Department of Biochemistry, The George Washington University Medical Center

**Publication Date:** 1984

**Summary:** The article describes a new procedure for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells using digitonin. The authors compare this method to the commonly used Nagarse method and report higher yield and respiratory control ratios with the digitonin method. They also investigate the respiratory characteristics of mitochondria within digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells.

1. "A new improved procedure for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells is described."
2. "The unique feature of this technique is the use of digitonin to make the cells susceptible to disruption by Teflon pestle/glass vessel homogenization."
3. "The yield and respiratory control ratios of mitochondria isolated by this method from murine Ehrlich ascites tumor cells and rat AS30-D ascites hepatoma cells are significantly better than those obtained for mitochondria isolated by the commonly employed Nagarse method which involves the use of proteolytic enzymes."
4. "Mitochondria isolated by this new procedure from three different lines of tumors exhibit respiratory control ratios with both adenosine diphosphate and a respiratory uncoupler comparable to those obtained with mitochondria present in situ within digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells."
5. "Several procedures for the isolation of mitochondria from ascites tumor cells have been described."
6. "Each of these has attempted to deal with the problem of the high resistance of these cells to disruption and homogenization."
7. "Reported methods for effective homogenization include grinding with glass beads, Dounce homogenization, nitrogen cavitation, sonication, and proteolytic digestion by Nagarse followed by Potter-Elvehjem homogenization."
8. "Each of these, including the most commonly used Nagarse method, have been criticized on the grounds that such harsh procedures produce variable degrees of mitochondrial damage."
9. "This report describes a new, relatively gentle method for disrupting ascites tumor cells by permeabilizing them with the steroid glycoside digitonin."
10. "Mitochondria isolated by this procedure exhibit a higher yield and higher respiratory control ratios than those obtained by the Nagarse procedure."
11. "Experiments are also described in which the respiratory control ratios of mitochondria present within digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells are determined."
12. "These values were found to be significantly higher than those obtained with isolated mitochondria prepared by the Nagarse procedure, but were comparable to those obtained with mitochondria isolated by the digitonin method."

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed description of a new method for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells using digitonin. The authors compare this method to the commonly used Nagarse method and report better yield and respiratory control ratios with the digitonin method. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for the isolation procedure, focusing more on the comparison and advantages of the new method. Additional information regarding the specific steps and conditions of the digitonin-permeabilization and subsequent isolation process is not provided.

summary:
Summary 4:
A new method for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells using digitonin is described. This technique improves the yield and respiratory control ratios compared to the Nagarse method. The procedure involves permeabilizing the cells with digitonin, resulting in higher yields and respiratory control ratios. The method is effective in disrupting tumor cells and allows for the study of abnormal mitochondrial activities and alterations in respiratory characteristics. The use of the steroid glycoside digitonin as a gentle method for disrupting ascites tumor cells and permeabilizing them is highlighted. Additionally, the respiratory characteristics of mitochondria within digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells are examined. The study focused on Ehrlich ascites tumor cells and Rat AS30-D ascites hepatoma cells. The researchers investigated the proteolytic enzymes and metabolic characteristics of tumor cells, particularly the regulatory coupling between glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation. This new method provides valuable insights into the respiratory characteristics of ascites cell mitochondria within digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells using the digitonin procedure:

1. Centrifuge the cells at 180 x g for 5 minutes.
2. Resuspend the cell pellet in the initial volume of wash medium.
3. Repeat centrifugation and resuspension 1 or 2 more times to remove erythrocytes.
4. Resuspend the washed pellets in H-medium containing 210 mM mannitol, 70 mM sucrose, 0.5% (w/v) BSA, 5 mM K-Hepes (pH 7.2), and 1.0 mM K-EGTA (pH 7.2).
5. Centrifuge at 625 x g for 5 minutes.
6. Resuspend the resulting pellets in the same medium to a concentration of 1-2 x 10^7 cells/ml.
7. Add aliquots of a 10% (w/v) solution of digitonin in dimethylsulfoxide to the cell suspension while stirring slowly in an ice bath.
8. Take small samples of cells after each addition of digitonin and determine their ability to exclude the stain trypan blue under a light microscope.
9. Stop the addition of digitonin when greater than 90% of the cells become permeabilized and no longer exclude the stain.
10. Double the volume of the cell suspension by adding H-medium (plus EGTA).
11. Centrifuge at 3000 x g for 3 minutes to obtain the permeabilized cell pellet.
12. Resuspend the cell pellet in H-medium (plus EGTA) to the original resuspended pellet volume.
13. Homogenize the suspension in approximately 30-ml aliquots using a tight-fitting Teflon pestle in a glass tube (Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer) with 10 up-and-down cycles at approximately 1500 rpm.
14. Verify cell disruption under light microscopy.
15. Triple the volume of the combined homogenates with H-medium (plus EGTA).
16. Remove cell debris by centrifugation at 625 x g for 5 minutes.
17. Carefully pour the supernatant, which contains the mitochondria, from the centrifuge tubes, avoiding any transfer of the gelatinous pellet.
18. Resuspend the H-medium pellet to 50% of the resuspended pellet volume.
19. Homogenize the H-medium pellet using 2-5 up-and-down cycles.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide details on the Nagarse procedure for isolating mitochondria.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from ascites tumor cells using the digitonin procedure. However, it does not describe the Nagarse procedure mentioned as an alternative method.

summary: Cells were resuspended and centrifuged multiple times to remove erythrocytes. All subsequent procedures were conducted at 0-4°C. For respiratory experiments, the final washed cell pellet was resuspended in a minimal volume of wash medium. For mitochondrial isolation, the washed pellets were resuspended in H-medium. Digitonin was added to permeabilize the cells, and the addition was stopped when over 90% of the cells were stained by Trypan Blue. After digitonin titration, the cell suspension volume was doubled, and centrifugation yielded the permeabilized cell pellet. The pellet was then resuspended and homogenized. The combined homogenates were tripled in volume, and cell debris was removed by centrifugation. The supernatant, containing the mitochondria, was separated from the pellet. The pellet was resuspended and homogenized again to increase the yield of mitochondrial protein.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from the article:

1. "This homogenate was centrifuged at 6251g for 5 min and the resulting supernatant was combined with that from the first homogenate."
2. "A crude mitochondrial pellet (MI) was then obtained by centrifugation of the pooled supernatants at 12,000g for 10 min."
3. "The resulting supernatant plus any gelatinous material present above the MI pellet was aspirated and the pellet was gently resuspended in H-medium (minus EGTA) to 50% of the previous volume."
4. "The second mitochondrial pellet (MZ) was then obtained by centrifugation at 9800g for 10 min."
5. "The resulting supernatant as well as any light 'fluffy-layer' mitochondria, was aspirated and the mitochondrial pellets were resuspended in the same medium to approximately 35-70 ml."
6. "The final mitochondrial pellet (M3) was obtained by centrifugation of the MZ resuspension at 9800g for 10 min in one or two 40-ml tubes."
7. "The supernatant was removed and the M3 pellet was gently resuspended with H-medium (minus EGTA) to a final volume of 1-3 ml."

Incompleteness or uncertainties:
- The article does not provide information on the composition of the H-medium or the specific centrifuge model used.
- The exact volume of the supernatant and the mitochondrial pellets after each centrifugation step is not mentioned.
- The article does not describe the specific method used for homogenization.

Conclusion:
The protocol for mitochondrial isolation provided in the article is incomplete and lacks some important details. The steps mentioned provide a general understanding of the isolation process, but the lack of information on medium composition and specific volumes makes it difficult to replicate the protocol accurately. Further information or a more detailed protocol would be needed to perform the mitochondrial isolation procedure accurately.

summary: Summary 4: The homogenate obtained from Ehrlich ascites tumor cells was centrifuged at 6251xg for 5 minutes, resulting in a mitochondrial pellet and supernatant. The supernatant was combined with the first homogenate. The crude mitochondrial pellet (MI) was obtained by centrifugation of the pooled supernatants at 12,000g for 10 minutes. The resulting supernatant, along with any gelatinous material, was aspirated, and the pellet was resuspended in H-medium. The second mitochondrial pellet (MZ) was obtained by centrifugation at 9800g for 10 minutes. The resulting supernatant, as well as any light "fluffy-layer" mitochondria, was aspirated, and the mitochondrial pellets were resuspended. The final mitochondrial pellet (M3) was obtained by centrifugation of the MZ resuspension at 9800g for 10 minutes. The supernatant was removed, and the M3 pellet was gently resuspended with H-medium. The protein concentration of the final mitochondrial suspension was normally 50-100 mg/ml. The average yields of mitochondrial protein for the three different lines of ascites tumor cells are given in Table 1. Oxygen consumption by cells and mitochondria was measured using a Clark oxygen electrode covered with an ultrathin Teflon membrane. The respiratory control of isolated ascites tumor mitochondria was assessed in the presence of oligomycin, and the acceptor control ratio (ACR) was calculated. The study also included measurements of electron transport and ATP synthesis. The protein concentration was determined using a modified biuret reaction with bovine serum albumin as the standard. Cell densities were determined using a Coulter counter. Various chemicals and reagents, including digitonin, were used in the experiments. The results of the acceptor control ratio (ACR) measurements are shown in Figure 2.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "A progressive increase in the rate of oxygen consumption was observed during consecutive additions of low concentrations of digitonin to the cell suspension."
2. "At a digitonin concentration of 0.010% (6.8 pg/ 1 O6 cells), the rate of uncoupled succinate oxidation by 22aH hepatoma cells was maximal."
3. "Further additions of digitonin (up to 0.080%) did not affect this rate, the state 3 rate, or the state 4 rate obtained in the presence of oligomycin."
4. "Once the concentration of digitonin providing the maximal rate of succinate-dependent respiration by the cells was established, respiratory control of the digitonin-permeabilized cells was determined."
5. "Mouse 22aH hepatoma cells were added to the respiratory medium in the presence of pyruvate + malate, ADP, and (0.010%) digitonin."
6. "A linear rate of state 3 respiration was obtained within 30 seconds after the addition of cells."
7. "The rate of non-phosphorylating respiration was obtained by the addition of oligomycin at a concentration that was previously determined to be optimal for the particular cell suspension (5-10 µg ml-1)."
8. "After a linear rate of resting respiration was established by this method, the maximal rate of respiration was induced by the addition of an optimal level of uncoupler (0.1-0.2 µM)."
9. "The acceptor control ratio (ACR) and respiratory control ratio (RCR) were determined in the same manner as that described for isolated mitochondria."
10. "The mean values (*SD) for the ACR and RCR of isolated tumor mitochondria and digitonin-permeabilized Ehrlich, AS30-D, and 22aH tumor cells are given in Table 2."
11. "For either pyruvate plus malate or succinate, the ACRs and RCRs of mitochondria isolated by the digitonin procedure were approximately twice those of mitochondria isolated by the Nagarse procedure."
12. "Analysis of the raw data indicated that this was primarily due to a relatively high rate of state 4 respiration by the Nagarse-prepared mitochondria."
13. "The respiratory control ratios of digitonin-treated Ehrlich ascites tumor cells for three different oxidizable substrates are provided in the third row of Table 2."

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in the isolation of digitonin-permeabilized ascites tumor mitochondria. The steps include determining the optimal concentration of digitonin, adding cells to the respiratory medium, measuring state 3 and state 4 respiration rates, and calculating the ACR and RCR. However, there are some protocol gaps, such as the specific composition of the respiratory medium and the exact method used for determining the ACR and RCR.

DISCUSSION 
The use of digitonin in the isolation of as- 
cites tumor mitochondria arose from previous 
studies describing the measurement of mito- 
chondrial activities within digitonin-perme- 
abilized cells ( 10,13-l 8) The development of 
the digitonin isolation procedure was also 
promoted by our early findings that the re- 
spiratory control of Nagarse-prepared Ehrlich 
and AS30-D tumor mitochondria was signif- 
icantly less than that of the corresponding 
permeabilized tumor cells (Table 2) 
The results of the respiratory experiments 
shown in Table 2 indicate that energy coupling 
in ascites tumor mitochondria isolated by the 
digitonin method is substantially better pre- 
served than in those prepared with Nagarse 
and is generally very similar to that of mi- 
tochondria present within nonhomogenized 
cells The ACRs and RCRs of these mito- 
chondria are also high and less variable than 
those reported by others using the Nagarse (6) 
or other ceil disruption techniques (2) In ad- 
dition, the mitochondrial yield is several times 
greater than we (Table I) and others (6) have 
obtained with the Nagarse procedure 
In summary, the new mitochondrial iso- 
lation technique described here offers several 
important advantages over other methods de- 
scribed for ascites tumor cells It remains to 
be established whether it has broader appli- 
cability in the isolation of mitochondria from 
other tissues that are difficult to completely 
homogenize without incurring mitochondrial 
damage The results of this and other studies 
also indicate that many mitochondrial activ- 
ities can be :juccessfully assayed in situ with 
suspensions ‘of isolated, digitonin-permeabil- 
ized cells This technique can be particularly useful when the amount of available tissue is 
insufficient to proceed with a standard iso- 
lation procedure for mitochondria

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The use of digitonin in the isolation of ascites tumor mitochondria arose from previous studies describing the measurement of mitochondrial activities within digitonin-permeabilized cells.
2. The development of the digitonin isolation procedure was promoted by early findings that the respiratory control of Nagarse-prepared Ehrlich and AS30-D tumor mitochondria was significantly less than that of the corresponding permeabilized tumor cells.
3. The results of the respiratory experiments indicate that energy coupling in ascites tumor mitochondria isolated by the digitonin method is substantially better preserved than in those prepared with Nagarse and is generally very similar to that of mitochondria present within nonhomogenized cells.
4. The acceptor control ratios (ACRs) and respiratory control ratios (RCRs) of the digitonin-isolated mitochondria are high and less variable than those reported using the Nagarse or other cell disruption techniques.
5. The mitochondrial yield is several times greater with the digitonin isolation procedure compared to the Nagarse procedure.
6. The new mitochondrial isolation technique offers several advantages over other methods for ascites tumor cells.
7. It remains to be established whether this technique has broader applicability in the isolation of mitochondria from other tissues that are difficult to completely homogenize without incurring mitochondrial damage.
8. Many mitochondrial activities can be successfully assayed in situ with suspensions of isolated, digitonin-permeabilized cells.
9. This technique can be particularly useful when the amount of available tissue is insufficient to proceed with a standard isolation procedure for mitochondria.

**Conclusion:**
The article discusses the use of digitonin in the isolation of ascites tumor mitochondria and compares the respiratory control ratios and acceptor control ratios obtained using the digitonin method with those obtained using the Nagarse method. The digitonin isolation procedure is found to better preserve energy coupling in the mitochondria and yield higher ACRs and RCRs compared to the Nagarse procedure. The article highlights the advantages of the digitonin isolation technique for ascites tumor cells and suggests its potential applicability for isolating mitochondria from other difficult-to-homogenize tissues. However, specific steps for the digitonin isolation procedure are not mentioned in the article.

The respiratory control ratios of digitonin-isolated mitochondria and permeabilized cells were also highly correlated for the 22aH hepatoma. The acceptor and respiratory control ratios for the tumor mitochondria were comparable to those commonly obtained with mitochondria from normal tissues. The use of digitonin in the isolation of ascites tumor mitochondria preserved energy coupling and yielded higher respiratory control ratios compared to the Nagarse method. This new isolation technique offers advantages in terms of energy preservation, yield, and applicability to other tissues.

hypothetical_questions.question: What were the similarities and differences in the values obtained with digitonin-prepared Ehrlich mitochondria, digitonin-prepared AS30-D mitochondria, and mitochondria isolated by the Nagarse method?
How did the acceptor control and respiratory control ratios for digitonin-prepared tumor mitochondria and digitonin-permeabilized tumor cells compare, particularly with succinate as a substrate?

Imagine a scenario where the Nagarse method was used instead of digitonin in the isolation of ascites tumor mitochondria. How might the energy preservation and respiratory control ratios differ in this scenario?

How do the acceptor and respiratory control ratios for the tumor mitochondria compare to those commonly obtained with mitochondria from normal tissues? What implications does this have for the study of tumor mitochondria?

How might the use of digitonin in the isolation of ascites tumor mitochondria impact the yield and applicability of this new isolation technique to other tissues? Can you think of any potential challenges or limitations that researchers might face in implementing this technique?

summary: Revised Summary: We extend our gratitude to Mrs. Tanya Hart and Mrs. Jocelyn Holzer for their invaluable technical assistance, as well as Jean Brereton for her secretarial work. We also acknowledge the contributions of Kaschnitz R.M., Scarpa A., Fiskum G., Skarkowska L., Estabrook R.W. (1967), Fiskum G., Harris S.I., Granger D.L., and Lehninger, Becker G.L., and the publications Abstracts of the 11th International Congress of Biology, Methods in Cell Biology, Biochimica et Biophysica Acta, Journal of General Physiology, Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America, Biochemistry, and FEBS Letters. Additionally, we would like to recognize Hullihen, Decker, Soper, Bustamante, Hatet, Morris, Graziotti, Craig, Klingenberg, Pullman, Villalobo, Balaban, Barrett, Mandel, Dubinsky, Cockrell, Estabrook, and the contributions of the journals Biochim Biophys, J Gen Physiol, Proc Natl Acad Sci USA, Fed Proc, Methods in Enzymology, Int Cong Bio, J Biol Chem, J Cell Biol, Methods Cell Biol, FEBS Lett, and the abstracts from the 11th International Congress of Biochemistry.
